1:
On James 4:1 — “From whence come wars and fightings among you? come they not hence, even of your lusts that war in your members?” — the witnesses mark the inward source of strife.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the question. Robertson: “whence” (pothen, twice) asks the origin; “wars” (polemoi) the chronic state, “fightings” (machai) the outbreaks. Ellicott: the contrast with the peace of chapter 3. The Puritan Henry: a lust after worldly things carried their divisions to a shameful height. Wesley: “your pleasures… war against your souls… the first seat of the war.”

The verse traces strife to its root. The wars and fightings among them spring from the lusts warring in their members. Outward conflict has an inward cause.

So the witnesses see James trace the conflicts among his readers back to their inward lusts. The witnesses note that these are not national wars but the quarrels, feuds, and contentions within the Christian community — the “wars” the settled state of hostility, the “fightings” its particular outbreaks. They mark the searching question: “come they not hence, even of your lusts?” — the true origin of strife is not chiefly the other party, but the cravings, the disordered desires for pleasure and gain, that wage war “in your members,” in the very faculties of body and soul. They observe Wesley’s point that these pleasures first war against the soul itself, and from that inward war proceeds the outward; the battlefield is the heart before it is the assembly. They note the contrast Ellicott draws with the heavenly wisdom of the preceding chapter, which is “peaceable”: where that wisdom makes peace, these earthly lusts make war. They observe Henry’s point that an overvaluing of worldly things had carried their divisions to a shameful height. The verse thus lays bare the root of contention: not circumstance but covetous desire — the lusts within that war in the members and break out in strife — a diagnosis the next verses press home.

So the witnesses see the strife among the readers traced to inward lust. These are not national wars but the quarrels within the community — the “wars” the settled hostility, the “fightings” its outbreaks. The searching question: “come they not hence, even of your lusts?” — the true origin is not the other party but the disordered cravings for pleasure and gain that war “in your members.” Wesley notes the pleasures first war against the soul itself, the heart the battlefield before the assembly. Where the heavenly wisdom of chapter 3 is peaceable, these earthly lusts make war; covetous desire, not circumstance, is the root of contention.
2:
On James 4:2 — “Ye lust, and have not: ye kill, and desire to have, and cannot obtain: ye fight and war, yet ye have not, because ye ask not” — the witnesses mark the fruitlessness of unbridled desire.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the escalation. Robertson: “ye lust” (epithymeite), coveting what one does not have. Barnes: you wish to have, and this prompts force. The Reformed Calvin: the soul of man indulging wicked lusts is insatiable. Wesley: “ye kill — in your heart, for he that hateth his brother is a murderer… ye ask not… a man full of evil desire cannot pray.”

The verse pictures craving that never satisfies. They lust, kill, fight, and war, yet do not obtain, because they do not ask God. Desire without prayer comes to nothing.

So the witnesses see James expose the restless, fruitless cycle of ungoverned desire. The witnesses note the escalation: “ye lust, and have not” — craving fiercely yet never satisfied; “ye kill, and desire to have, and cannot obtain” — the desire so violent it is murderous (whether, as Wesley reads it, killing in the heart by hatred, or driving to deadly extremes), yet still it fails of its object; “ye fight and war, yet ye have not” — all the striving leaves them empty-handed. They mark Calvin’s point that wicked lust is insatiable: he who lets sinful desire rule will know no end to it, for were the world given him he would crave another. They observe the decisive diagnosis: “ye have not, because ye ask not” — the real reason for their emptiness is that they do not bring their desires to God in prayer, but seek to seize by force what only God can give. They note Wesley’s pointed observation that a man full of evil desire, envy, or hatred cannot truly pray. They observe that the failure is thus self-inflicted: grasping replaces asking, and grasping comes to nothing. The verse thus shows the bankruptcy of covetous striving: all the lusting, killing, and fighting yield nothing, because the one thing needful — humble asking of God — is left undone.
3:
On James 4:3 — “Ye ask, and receive not, because ye ask amiss, that ye may consume it upon your lusts” — the witnesses mark the failure of selfish prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the fault. Robertson: “ye ask amiss” (kakos aiteisthe), asking for yourselves and evilly; “that ye may spend it in your pleasures” (hedonais). Barnes: men who go to war sometimes pray, yet for wrong ends. The Reformed Calvin: they wished to make God the minister of their own lusts, giving unbridled license to their wishes.

The verse answers the case of those who do ask. They receive not because they ask amiss, to spend on their pleasures. Prayer aimed at self-indulgence is refused.

So the witnesses see James meet the objection of those who do pray yet go unanswered. The witnesses note the concession: “ye ask” — some do pray; yet “receive not.” They mark the reason: “because ye ask amiss” — the prayer is wrongly made, asked “evilly,” with a corrupt aim. They observe the specific corruption: “that ye may consume it upon your lusts” — they ask God’s gifts not for his glory or genuine need, but to squander on their pleasures, their sensual indulgence. They note Calvin’s sharp point that such petitioners would make God the minister of their own lusts, setting no bounds to their wishes; they ask that he would serve their appetites. They observe that this completes the previous verse: it is not enough to ask — one must ask rightly, for God will not lend his bounty to feed sin. They note that prayer offered to fuel self-indulgence carries its own refusal: God, who gives good gifts, will not be made the supplier of vice. The verse thus closes the diagnosis of failed desire: even when they pray, they fail, because they pray amiss, seeking only to gratify their lusts — the very lusts that, the next verse warns, have made them friends of the world and enemies of God.

So the witnesses see James answer those who do pray yet go unanswered. The concession: “ye ask” — some do pray; yet “receive not.” The reason: “because ye ask amiss” — asked “evilly,” with a corrupt aim. The specific corruption: “that ye may consume it upon your lusts” — they ask God’s gifts not for his glory but to squander on their pleasures. Calvin’s sharp point: such petitioners would make God the minister of their own lusts, setting no bounds to their wishes. It is not enough to ask — one must ask rightly, for God will not lend his bounty to feed sin. Prayer offered to fuel self-indulgence carries its own refusal.
4:
On James 4:4 — “Ye adulterers and adulteresses, know ye not that the friendship of the world is enmity with God?” — the witnesses mark the incompatibility of the world and God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure and the text. Robertson: the true reading is “ye adulteresses” (moichalides), the masculine spurious; whether literal or figurative for unfaithfulness to God. Ellicott: those who have broken their marriage vow to God. Adam Clarke: Israel’s covenant with God made idolatry adultery. Wesley: “the love of the world… is enmity against God.”

The verse names worldliness as spiritual adultery. Friendship with the world is enmity with God. To love the world is to betray him.

So the witnesses see James brand worldliness as spiritual adultery and set it in flat opposition to God. The witnesses note the startling address: “ye adulteresses” (the better text, the feminine) — not, the expositors agree, charging literal adultery, but using the prophets’ familiar figure: as Israel was espoused to God, so to love the world is to be unfaithful to one’s divine husband, to commit spiritual adultery. They mark the principle: “the friendship of the world is enmity with God” — to court the world’s favor, to set the heart on its pleasures, riches, and approval, is to be at war with God; the two loves cannot coexist. They observe that “friendship of the world” means (as Wesley defines from John) the desire of the flesh and eyes and the pride of life, the courting of worldly men’s favor. They note the absolute terms: not coolness merely but “enmity,” active hostility; the worldling is, in effect, an enemy of God. They observe that this exposes the deep root of the strife and selfish prayer just condemned: a heart wedded to the world. The verse thus delivers the chapter’s central charge: love of the world is adultery against God and enmity with him — a betrayal made worse, the next verse adds, against the Spirit who jealously yearns over us.

So the witnesses see James brand worldliness as spiritual adultery and set it against God. The startling address “ye adulteresses” (the better text) is not literal but the prophets’ figure: as Israel was espoused to God, so to love the world is to be unfaithful to one’s divine husband. The principle: “the friendship of the world is enmity with God” — to court the world’s favour, pleasures, and approval is to be at war with God; the two loves cannot coexist. Wesley defines worldly friendship as the desire of the flesh and eyes and the pride of life. Not coolness but active “enmity”: the worldling is, in effect, an enemy of God — the deep root of the strife condemned.
5:
On James 4:5 — “Do ye think that the scripture saith in vain, The spirit that dwelleth in us lusteth to envy?” — the witnesses mark one of the epistle’s most debated verses.

The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with it. Robertson: “no O.T. passage is precisely like this,” a poetic rendering of Exodus 20:5 or the thought of Genesis 6:5; cf. Galatians 5:17. Ellicott renders it of the Holy Spirit “that dwelleth in us” who “jealously regards us as His own.” Adam Clarke: “exceedingly obscure.” Wesley: the spirit of love “lusteth against envy.” The Reformed Calvin refers it back to the worldliness condemned.

The verse cites Scripture on the spirit and envy. Whether it speaks of man’s envious spirit or God’s jealous Spirit is much disputed. Either way it reinforces the call from worldliness.

So the witnesses approach a verse the expositors freely confess to be among the hardest in the epistle. The witnesses note the first difficulty: the writer appeals to what “the scripture saith,” yet no Old Testament text reads exactly thus; most take it as a free, poetic summary of the general teaching of Scripture (as in the second commandment, or Genesis) that God is jealous and man’s heart prone to envy, rather than a quotation of one verse. They mark the deeper, debated question of whose “spirit” is meant. On one reading, “the spirit that dwelleth in us” is the human spirit, the natural bent of man, which “lusteth to envy” — tends toward envious, grasping desire (suiting the context of worldly strife). On the other reading, favored by Ellicott and many, “the Spirit” is the Holy Spirit whom God has made to dwell in believers, who “jealously yearns” over us, longing for our undivided love — so that the verse rebukes the spiritual adultery just named. They observe Wesley’s related sense that the spirit of love in believers strives against envy (Galatians 5:17). They note that both readings serve the argument: whether it exposes man’s envious bent or God’s jealous love, it presses the readers from the world to God. The verse thus, however construed, undergirds the warning: Scripture is not silent — the matter of the indwelling spirit and envy bears directly on the call to forsake worldly friendship, which the next verse answers with grace.
6:
On James 4:6 — “But he giveth more grace. Wherefore he saith, God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace unto the humble” — the witnesses mark the greater grace given to the humble.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the quotation. Robertson: “more grace” (meizona charin), greater grace for the greater requirement, quoting Proverbs 3:34; “God resisteth the proud” (antitassetai, sets in battle array). Adam Clarke: a greater benefit than all the world can bestow. Wesley: “pride is the great root of all unkind affections.”

The verse turns from rebuke to grace. God gives greater grace, resisting the proud but giving grace to the humble. Humility is the channel of grace.

So the witnesses see James, having exposed the deep corruption of worldliness, turn to the abundance of God’s grace. The witnesses note the gracious “but”: against the strength of indwelling sin and the pull of the world, “he giveth more grace” — greater grace, more than sufficient for the greater need; God’s supply outstrips the demand. They mark the citation of Proverbs: “God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace unto the humble” — the great law of God’s dealings. They observe the first half: “God resisteth the proud” — he sets himself in battle array against the haughty, arraying his power against them; pride is precisely the spirit of the worldly striving just condemned. They note the second: “giveth grace unto the humble” — the lowly, who own their need and submit to God, are the very ones to whom grace flows. They observe Wesley’s point that pride is the root of all the unkind, envious affections James has been rebuking; humility is its cure. They note that this verse becomes the hinge of the chapter, grounding the string of commands that follow (submit, draw near, humble yourselves) in the promise that grace is given to the humble. The verse thus opens the way of recovery: God gives greater grace, but only to the humble — resisting the proud — so that the path back to him, the next verses show, runs through humble submission.

So the witnesses see James turn from rebuke to the abundance of grace. The gracious “but”: against indwelling sin and the pull of the world, “he giveth more grace” — greater grace, more than sufficient; God’s supply outstrips the demand. The citation of Proverbs: “God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace unto the humble.” The first half: God sets himself in battle array against the haughty, the spirit of the worldly striving just condemned. The second: grace flows to the lowly who own their need and submit. Wesley notes pride is the root of all the envious affections James rebukes; humility is its cure. This becomes the hinge of the chapter, grounding the commands that follow in the promise that grace is given to the humble.
7:
On James 4:7 — “Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you” — the witnesses mark the double command of submission and resistance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the verbs. Robertson: “be subject” (hypotagete, ingressive aorist, military term, to range under); “resist” (antistete). Adam Clarke: God never permits the devil to conquer the man who continues to resist. The Reformed Calvin: the submission of humility, for the Spirit rests on the humble. Wesley: “by humbly submitting… resist the devil, the father of pride.”

The verse gives the first steps of recovery. Submit to God; resist the devil, and he will flee. Yielding to God and standing against Satan go together.

So the witnesses see James draw from the promise of grace to the humble a double charge. The witnesses note the “therefore”: because God gives grace to the humble, the way of recovery begins with humility. They mark the first command: “submit yourselves… to God” — a military word, to range oneself under God’s command, to yield wholly to his will and authority; this is the submission of the humble heart on which grace rests. They observe the second: “resist the devil” — to stand firm against the tempter, who is the instigator of the pride and worldliness condemned. They note the encouraging promise: “and he will flee from you” — Satan, met with steadfast resistance, retreats; as Clarke observes, God never permits the devil to conquer the man who keeps resisting, for he cannot force the will. They observe the order and balance: submission to God comes first and empowers the resistance; one cannot stand against the devil except by yielding to God. They note Wesley’s point that the devil is the father of the very pride and envy the chapter rebukes, so resisting him is of a piece with forsaking the world. The verse thus charts the first movements of return: yield to God in humility, and from that footing resist the devil — who, so resisted, flees; and then, the next verse adds, draw near to God.

So the witnesses see James draw a double charge from the promise of grace to the humble. The “therefore”: because God gives grace to the humble, recovery begins with humility. The first command: “submit yourselves to God” — a military word, to range oneself under God’s command, the submission of the humble heart on which grace rests. The second: “resist the devil” — stand firm against the tempter, instigator of the pride condemned. The promise: “he will flee from you” — Satan, met with steadfast resistance, retreats; as Clarke observes, God never lets the devil conquer the man who keeps resisting, for he cannot force the will. Submission to God comes first and empowers the resistance.
8:
On James 4:8 — “Draw nigh to God, and he will draw nigh to you. Cleanse your hands, ye sinners; and purify your hearts, ye double minded” — the witnesses mark the call to approach God in cleansing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the priestly imagery. Robertson: “draw nigh” (eggisate), as the priests drew nigh (Exodus 19:22); “cleanse your hands” the outward, “purify your hearts” the inward. Ellicott: like the father of the prodigal, God runs to meet us (Luke 15:20). Wesley: “cease from doing evil… purify your hearts… be no more double minded.”

The verse promises God’s nearness to those who approach. Draw near to God and he draws near; cleanse hands and purify hearts. Approach and purity belong together.

So the witnesses see James issue a gracious invitation joined to a demand for cleansing. The witnesses note the great promise: “draw nigh to God, and he will draw nigh to you” — if we approach God in faith and prayer, he meets us; as Clarke and Ellicott put it, when the soul sets out to seek God, God sets out to meet the soul, like the father running to the returning prodigal. They mark that this both invites and assures: God is not reluctant but ready, waiting to be gracious. They observe the conditions of acceptable approach, drawn from the priestly washings: “cleanse your hands, ye sinners” — the outward life, the deeds (the hands), must be cleansed from evil-doing; “and purify your hearts, ye double minded” — the inward man, the heart, must be purged of its divided allegiance. They note the two names of rebuke: “sinners,” for the foul hands; “double minded,” for the divided heart that tries to love both God and the world. They observe Wesley’s point that this means ceasing from evil and ending the double-mindedness of serving two masters. The verse thus shows how to draw near: God will meet the returning soul, but the approach demands cleansed hands and a purified, undivided heart — a thoroughgoing repentance the next verse deepens into mourning.

So the witnesses see James join a gracious invitation to a demand for cleansing. The promise: “draw nigh to God, and he will draw nigh to you” — when the soul sets out to seek God, God sets out to meet it, like the father running to the prodigal. God is not reluctant but ready, waiting to be gracious. The conditions, from the priestly washings: “cleanse your hands, ye sinners” — the outward deeds purged from evil; “purify your hearts, ye double minded” — the inward man purged of divided allegiance. The two names rebuke: “sinners” for the foul hands, “double minded” for the divided heart trying to love both God and the world. The approach demands cleansed hands and an undivided heart.
9:
On James 4:9 — “Be afflicted, and mourn, and weep: let your laughter be turned to mourning, and your joy to heaviness” — the witnesses mark the call to godly sorrow.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the repentance. Robertson: “be afflicted” (talaiporesate, endure misery), “mourn” (penthesate). Ellicott: wretchedness, sorrow, and tears are “the three steps of the homeward way to peace and God.” The Reformed Calvin: a salutary mourning that leads to repentance, alluding to Luke 6:25. Adam Clarke: many had lived dissolute lives.

The verse calls for sorrow over sin. Be afflicted, mourn, and weep; turn laughter to mourning. Carnal mirth must give way to penitent grief.

So the witnesses see James summon his worldly readers to godly sorrow for their sin. The witnesses note the threefold call: “be afflicted, and mourn, and weep” — not a bare outward show, but real inward grief over their sins, expressing itself in genuine sorrow and tears; as Ellicott beautifully puts it, wretchedness, sorrow, and tears are the three steps of the homeward way to peace and God. They mark the reversal demanded: “let your laughter be turned to mourning, and your joy to heaviness” — the carnal mirth and worldly gaiety in which they had lived must be exchanged for penitent sadness; the laughter of the careless sinner is to become the mourning of the contrite. They observe the allusion (Calvin) to Christ’s woe on those who laugh now, and the call to that salutary mourning which works repentance. They note that this is not a summons to gloom for its own sake, but to the godly sorrow that, owning the gravity of sin, breaks the heart and turns it to God. They observe that the worldly, who had lived in pleasure, must feel the bitterness of their offense before they can know the joy of pardon. The verse thus presses repentance to its depth: the friends of the world must mourn and weep over their unfaithfulness, turning their hollow laughter to grief — a humbling that, the next verse promises, God will answer by lifting them up.
10:
On James 4:10 — “Humble yourselves in the sight of the Lord, and he shall lift you up” — the witnesses mark the promise to the humble.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the echo. Robertson: “humble yourselves” (tapeinothete, passive with reflexive sense), as Matthew 18:4; cf. 1 Peter 5:6. Ellicott: God dwells with the contrite and humble (Isaiah 57:15). Adam Clarke: submission to God’s authority precedes humiliation of soul. The Reformed Calvin: God’s peculiar work is to raise up the lowly.

The verse gathers the commands into a promise. Humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will lift you up. Self-abasement before God ends in exaltation.

So the witnesses see James sum up the call to repentance in a single command crowned with a promise. The witnesses note the command: “humble yourselves in the sight of the Lord” — take the lowly place before God, owning one’s sin, meanness, and unworthiness, bowing in genuine contrition; the “in the sight of the Lord” marks it as real humility before God, not show before men. They mark that this gathers up the preceding charges — submit, draw near, cleanse, mourn — into their root: humility. They observe the great promise: “and he shall lift you up” — God exalts the humble, raising them from their low estate to favor, dignity, and joy; the way down is the way up. They note Calvin’s point that it is God’s peculiar work to raise the lowly, fulfilling the law of verse 6 that he gives grace to the humble. They observe the echo of Christ’s own teaching that whoever humbles himself shall be exalted, and the close parallel in Peter. They note that this is the gospel paradox set against the worldly striving for eminence that began the chapter: men fought to exalt themselves and gained nothing; the humble who abase themselves before God are lifted by him. The verse thus crowns the call to repentance with its promise: humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will exalt you — before James turns to a particular fruit of pride, evil-speaking.
11:
On James 4:11 — “Speak not evil one of another, brethren. He that speaketh evil of his brother… speaketh evil of the law, and judgeth the law” — the witnesses mark the sin of evil-speaking.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “speak not against” (me katalaleite), a habit to be stopped. The Puritan Henry: katalaleite means speaking anything to hurt or injure another. Ellicott: do not “back-bite.” The Reformed Calvin: hypocrisy is presumptuous, exalting self by calumniating others. Wesley: he that speaks evil of his brother in effect speaks evil of the law.

The verse forbids slander among brethren. To speak evil of a brother is to speak evil of and judge the law. Backbiting usurps the law’s place.

So the witnesses see James turn to a particular fruit of pride: evil-speaking against the brethren. The witnesses note the command: “speak not evil one of another” — do not slander, backbite, or speak injuriously of a fellow-Christian; the tender address “brethren” reinforces the wrong of it among those bound in one family. They mark Calvin’s diagnosis that this springs from the same root as the chapter’s other sins: a proud hypocrisy that exalts self by disparaging others. They observe the subtle argument James makes: “he that speaketh evil of his brother… speaketh evil of the law, and judgeth the law” — the law commands love of neighbor; so the one who slanders his brother in effect condemns and sets himself above the law that forbids it, treating it as not worth keeping. They note that the slanderer thus arrogates to himself a position over the law — not its doer but its judge. They observe that this makes evil-speaking no light fault but a usurpation, an affront to the law and so to the Lawgiver. They note that the censorious tongue, condemning others, presumes to a seat of judgment that is not its own. The verse thus exposes the gravity of slander: to speak evil of a brother is to judge and despise the law of love — a presumption the next verse rebukes by naming the one true Lawgiver and Judge.
12:
On James 4:12 — “There is one lawgiver, who is able to save and to destroy: who art thou that judgest another?” — the witnesses mark the sole Lawgiver and Judge.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the title. Robertson: “one” (heis) excludes all others; “the lawgiver” (ho nomothetes); “to save… to destroy” (Matthew 10:28). Ellicott: God the ultimate source of law, all judgment delegated; “who art thou…?” Adam Clarke notes the addition “and judge” in many manuscripts. Wesley: “who art thou — a poor, weak, dying worm.”

The verse names the one Judge. There is one Lawgiver, able to save and destroy; who are you to judge another? Only God has the right the slanderer usurps.

So the witnesses see James rebuke the censorious by pointing to the one true Lawgiver and Judge. The witnesses note the singularity: “there is one lawgiver” (many texts add “and judge”) — God alone is the source and giver of the law and the rightful judge of those who break it; no creature shares this office. They mark his unique power: “who is able to save and to destroy” — God holds in his hand the eternal destiny of every soul, to save or to destroy; the judgment that matters belongs to him alone. They observe the withering question: “who art thou that judgest another?” — what right has the slanderer, a mere creature, to set himself in God’s seat and pass condemning judgment on his neighbor? Wesley sharpens it: who art thou, a poor, weak, dying worm, to usurp the throne of the Almighty Judge? They note that this is the ground of the prohibition of evil-speaking: to condemn a brother is to presume to an authority that belongs to God only. They observe that the verse humbles the proud tongue by setting it before the one Lawgiver who can save and destroy. The verse thus seals the rebuke of slander: God alone is Lawgiver and Judge, with power over life and death; the man who judges his brother forgets both his own creatureliness and God’s sole right — before James turns to another form of presumption, that of the self-confident planner.
13:
On James 4:13 — “Go to now, ye that say, To day or to morrow we will go into such a city, and continue there a year, and buy and sell, and get gain” — the witnesses mark the presumption of the self-confident planner.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rebuke. Robertson: “go to now” (age nyn), interjection (also James 5:1). Barnes: a presumptuous confidence about the future, forming plans without regard to God. Ellicott: the merchant absorbed in the quest of gain. The Reformed Calvin: they settled their plans as if they had many years at their disposal.

The verse confronts those who plan presumptuously. They say they will travel, trade, and profit, as if the future were theirs. Such confident planning ignores God.

So the witnesses see James turn from the proud tongue to the proud planner. The witnesses note the arresting address: “go to now” (or “come now”) — a call to attention, summoning the self-confident to hear. They mark the picture of presumption: “ye that say, To day or to morrow we will go into such a city, and continue there a year, and buy and sell, and get gain” — men who map out the future in confident detail, fixing the time, the place, the duration, the business, and the profit, as though all lay within their own control. They observe that the fault is not in trade or planning as such, but in the spirit of it: planning “as if,” as Calvin says, they had many years at their disposal, settling the future without any reference to God’s will or providence. They note Barnes’s point that this is a presumptuous confidence about the morrow, leaving God out of the reckoning. They observe Ellicott’s portrait of the merchant absorbed in the quest of gain, his heart set on getting. They note that such planning, however shrewd, forgets the most basic fact of human life — its utter dependence on God. The verse thus sets up the rebuke of presumption: the confident planner who lays out his future as his own — forgetting, as the next verse insists, that he knows nothing of the morrow and is but a vapor.
14:
On James 4:14 — “Whereas ye know not what shall be on the morrow. For what is your life? It is even a vapour, that appeareth for a little time, and then vanisheth away” — the witnesses mark the brevity of life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “ye who do not know” (ouk epistasthe) the morrow; life a “vapour” (atmis), a mist. Adam Clarke: life is utterly precarious; this should be read in a parenthesis. Barnes: they formed plans as if they knew, but it could not be known. The brevity of life rebukes the presumption.

The verse exposes the planner’s ignorance. They know not the morrow, and their life is a vapour soon gone. Such frailty makes confident planning folly.

So the witnesses see James puncture the planner’s presumption with two humbling facts. The witnesses note the first: “ye know not what shall be on the morrow” — the confident schemer cannot foresee even the next day; he plans a year ahead while wholly ignorant of tomorrow. They mark the searching question: “for what is your life?” — turning the man to consider the very ground on which all his plans rest. They observe the answer in its vivid figure: “it is even a vapour, that appeareth for a little time, and then vanisheth away” — human life is like a mist or puff of breath, visible a moment and then gone; frail, fleeting, and wholly uncertain. They note Clarke’s point that life is utterly precarious, not within any man’s power to secure. They observe that these two truths — ignorance of the morrow and the vapor-brevity of life — together expose the folly of planning as though the future were owned: how absurd to dispose confidently of years when one cannot command the next hour, and is himself a mist soon to vanish. They note that this is not meant to paralyze but to humble, to bring God into the reckoning. The verse thus lays bare the planner’s frailty: ignorant of tomorrow and fleeting as a vapor, he has no ground for his confident boasting — and ought rather, the next verse says, to submit his plans to the Lord’s will.
15:
On James 4:15 — “For that ye ought to say, If the Lord will, we shall live, and do this, or that” — the witnesses mark the proper submission of plans to God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the correction. Robertson: “instead of your saying” (anti tou legein hymas); “if the Lord will” (ean ho kyrios thele). Barnes: recognise absolute dependence on God for life and success. The Reformed Calvin: a twofold condition — if we live, and if the Lord will. Adam Clarke notes the rabbinic parallels.

The verse supplies the right alternative. They ought to say, If the Lord will, we shall live and do this. All plans are to be held under God’s will.

So the witnesses see James give the antidote to presumption: the submission of every plan to God’s will. The witnesses note the correction: “for that ye ought to say” — instead of the confident boasting just rebuked, this is the language proper to a creature; the right attitude is voiced. They mark the words: “If the Lord will, we shall live, and do this, or that” — a twofold acknowledgment, as Calvin notes: first, “if we live,” owning that our very continuance depends on God; and second, “if the Lord will,” owning that the success of all we undertake lies in his hand. They observe that this is not a mere formula to be tacked onto speech, but, as Barnes insists, a genuine recognition of absolute dependence on God for life and for the outcome of every effort. They note that such submission does not forbid planning, but sanctifies it, holding all our purposes under the overruling will of God. They observe that the believer who lives by this is freed from the anxious presumption of the worldling and rests his future in surer hands. They note that the spirit, not just the words, is what God regards. The verse thus supplies the cure for presumptuous confidence: to lay every plan before God with “if the Lord will” — the humble dependence the boasters, as the next verse shows, had abandoned for an evil glorying.
16:
On James 4:16 — “But now ye rejoice in your boastings: all such rejoicing is evil” — the witnesses mark the evil of self-confident boasting.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “in your vauntings” (alazoniais), braggart talk (also 1 John 2:16); “glorying” (kauchesis), evil here. Ellicott: the same word as “the pride of life.” Adam Clarke: boasting independence of God is impious. The Reformed Calvin: they flattered themselves, robbing God of his government.

The verse condemns their boasting outright. They rejoice in their vaunting, and all such rejoicing is evil. Confident self-reliance is sin, not prudence.

So the witnesses see James pronounce a flat condemnation on the spirit of presumptuous boasting. The witnesses note the contrast: “but now” — instead of the humble “if the Lord will,” they do the opposite; “ye rejoice in your boastings” — they take pride and pleasure in their confident, self-reliant schemes, vaunting their plans and powers as though the future were secured by their own wisdom. They mark the word for “boastings,” the same that John calls “the pride of life” — the empty, arrogant self-glorying of those who reckon without God. They observe the absolute verdict: “all such rejoicing is evil” — not merely unwise or imprudent, but morally evil, impious; for, as Calvin says, it robs God of his government, and Clarke adds, it is the boast of independence from the Almighty. They note that the sin lies not in joy itself but in the object of it: to glory in one’s own self-sufficiency, as if life and success did not hang on God, is wickedness. They observe that this exposes the heart of the matter — the planner’s confidence was not innocent foresight but proud independence. The verse thus condemns the boaster: his self-confident glorying is not shrewdness but sin — leading to the closing principle that to know the right and not do it is itself transgression.

So the witnesses see James pronounce a flat condemnation on presumptuous boasting. The contrast: instead of the humble “if the Lord will,” “ye rejoice in your boastings” — taking pride in confident, self-reliant schemes, vaunting their plans as though the future were secured by their own wisdom. The word for “boastings” is the same John calls “the pride of life” — the empty self-glorying of those who reckon without God. The absolute verdict: “all such rejoicing is evil” — not merely unwise but morally evil, impious; for, as Calvin says, it robs God of his government, and Clarke adds, it is the boast of independence from the Almighty. The sin lies not in joy but in its object: glorying in one’s own self-sufficiency.
17:
On James 4:17 — “Therefore to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin” — the witnesses mark the sin of known duty left undone.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the principle. Robertson: “to him that knoweth” (eidoti) how to do good and does it not. Barnes: “the ability to do good in any case imposes an obligation to do it” (Cotton Mather). Adam Clarke: after this warning none can plead ignorance. Wesley: knowledge does not prevent but increases his condemnation.

The verse states a broad principle of sin. To know the good and not do it is sin. Knowledge of duty makes its neglect guilt.

So the witnesses see James close the chapter with a weighty general principle of sin. The witnesses note the “therefore”: it draws partly on the immediate matter — having now been taught to say “if the Lord will” and to depend on God, they can no longer plead ignorance; but the principle is broad and applies to all known duty. They mark the statement: “to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin” — the one who knows what is right and yet fails to do it is guilty of sin; the knowledge of duty makes the omission culpable. They observe the searching reach of this: sin is not only doing what is forbidden, but failing to do the good one knows one ought — the sin of omission, not only of commission. They note Cotton Mather’s maxim, cited by Barnes, that the ability to do good in any case imposes an obligation to do it. They observe Wesley’s sobering point that knowledge, far from excusing, increases the condemnation of the one who knows yet does not obey; light brings responsibility. They note that this turns the chapter’s teaching into a personal reckoning: now that they know, neglect is guilt. The verse thus seals the chapter with a principle that searches every reader: to know the good and leave it undone is itself sin — a standard by which all the foregoing exhortations become binding duty.
